does: This paper applies sparse autoencoders (SAEs) to mechanistically interpret how Transformer-based time series forecasters like PatchTST build their internal representations. The authors train SAEs on post-GELU FFN activations with varying dictionary sizes (0.5x to 4.0x dimensionality) and find that expanding dictionaries yields negligible performance changes, with large portions of overcomplete dictionaries remaining inactive. Critically, they show that single-layer narrow-dimensional transformers match deeper configurations on standard benchmarks, and causal interventions targeting dominant latent features produce minimal forecast perturbation. These findings suggest time series forecasting does not rely on superposition—the compression of multiple concepts into individual neurons—contrary to mechanisms observed in language models.
connects: This work directly engages with the interpretability literature on transformers, building on recent applications of sparse autoencoders to understand neural network representations developed in mechanistic interpretability research. It responds to the persistent puzzle in time series forecasting: why simple linear models like DLinear remain competitive with complex transformers despite transformers' dominance in NLP. The paper extends mechanistic interpretability tools originally developed for language models to the time series domain, while connecting to ongoing debates about whether transformer architectures truly leverage their theoretical capacity or if inductive biases toward linear behavior suffice for forecasting tasks.
enables: This work opens avenues for designing more efficient time series forecasting architectures by clarifying which representational capacities are actually needed. If superposition is unnecessary for forecasting, future work could develop specialized architectures that avoid the computational overhead of high-dimensional transformations, potentially combining linear components with targeted nonlinearities only where mechanistic analysis shows they matter. The findings suggest that time series and NLP may fundamentally differ in their representational requirements, prompting investigation into what structural properties of sequences drive these differences. Additionally, this framework could guide pruning, distillation, and compression strategies by identifying which model components genuinely contribute to forecasting performance versus those that remain dormant.
